pr_review_comment_body: Oh, good catch, I'll remove that!

I considered that alternative as well. However, after noticing that some multiline strings also have trailing whitespace (see 432878bd773319cf034d3070697e8601d4201b6d), I thought it would be easier to just use `# noqa` to ignore both the whitespace errors and the indent errors. My thought process was that it wouldn't be good to edit the string to remove the trailing whitespace, since it was likely pasted from a terminal and we should preserve its formatting. As far as I know, there's no way to have flake8 ignore _just_ the trailing whitespace in multiline strings while still emitting indent errors for them, unfortunately.

Of course, preserving trailing whitespace inside multiline strings may not be all that important (it's hard to imagine a rule that relies on trailing whitespace for matching to work correctly), so maybe we should remove it, remove the `# noqa` comments, and indent the leading triple quotes as you're suggesting. Here's a commit showing what that might look like: https://github.com/josephfrazier/thefuck/commit/25efd1b670c8f9694f6e5617fe4c38666f9b0704. I'm not sure if I like the way [tests/rules/test_fix_file.py](https://github.com/josephfrazier/thefuck/blob/25efd1b670c8f9694f6e5617fe4c38666f9b0704/tests/rules/test_fix_file.py) looks, though :/

I disagree, if we create a new view of the `Video` every time I call `captureStream`, then I will lose synchronicity between `VideoStream` views, because they don't rely on the same `Video` view. This means that the `play` trait won't make any sense, and if I record a `VideoStream` with `VideoRecorder` I won't know which view is recorded.

Speaking about synchronicity, we discussed with Sylvain this morning about Video widget, and we agreed on the fact that the current implementation of  `VideoStream` should be the implementation of the `Video` widget. We currently don't have synchronicity between views for the `Video` widget, but if we have only one `video` element as part of the model, and the views are only streams of this `video` element, then we have synchronicity. And then it makes sense to have a `play` and `progress` traits in `Video` widget, which doesn't make sense right now.

when you add items to a vector, when it runs out of space for the items (which generally happens on the second item sometimes), it allocates memory for 2x the current number of items and then copies all of the items (copy constructor or move, depending on a number of things), into to the new space and then frees the old space. this can happen over and over again, and the whole reallocate and copy can be very expensive. they tend to even show up as hotspots in vtune. so if you know how many items a vector may have, it’s always a good idea to call reserve() because otherwise you are guaranteed a lot of extra reallocing And copying.

By default `$(...)` only captures stdout not stderr, so for a fresh provision this step currently outputs:
`./vagrant/setup.sh: line 108: geckodriver: command not found`

To prevent this, I'd redirect stderr to stdout like for the installation of pip above. I'd also quote the variables, so something like:
`if [[ "$(geckodriver --version 2>&1)" != *"${GECKODRIVER_VERSION}"* ]]; then`

I'm looking forward to using Docker where we won't have to do this version checking boilerplate and also don't have the duplication between this script and `travis-setup.sh` :-)
(It didn't seem worth trying to overcomplicate bug 1414094 by making it also consolidate the duplication, given both scripts will be replaced by the Docker bootstrap process)

You have several similar comments in this page. Please allow me to respond them here in one place. Yes I am aware of the "canned policy" and "custom policy" difference. In my understanding, the name "custom policy" is misleading in the first place. Customer can not really customize an arbitrary policy (just like what they can do in IAM policies), and expects the API to just sign it. [CloudFront only accepts "custom policy" with combinations of predefined 3 parameters in one format](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudFront/latest/DeveloperGuide/private-content-creating-signed-url-custom-policy.html#private-content-custom-policy-statement). So I can not see a scenario that customer would need more flexibility to build the policy by themselves and call a separated sign_custom_policy() function.

In other words, despite of the subtle output difference of so-called "canned policy" and "custom policy", their input are all driven by a subset of same parameters. There won't be a need to expose a public sign_custom_policy() for a "customized" policy.

I omitted ConcordanceIndex because a) Janez was totally into building our own model and I thought creating a method was part of it and b) I couldn't for the life of me figure out what ConcordanceIndex returns.
Until now.
It returns a dict with unique tokens as keys and a list of indexes of the token as values.
E.g.
text = "This is some text. This is another text."
c = ConcordanceIndex(text.tokens) #-ish
c = {"this": [0, 4], "is": [1, 5], "some": [2]....}
Totally valuable.

Usually, the learning rate is set when the optimizer is instantiated. But here, it is set to its corresponding value in each iteration.

Let's presume this case:
1. Select stochastic -> Vanilla SGD selected by default, with learning rate == 1.0
2. Step/Run -> Learning rate = self.learner.alpha (In this way, a user can change the learning rate at every moment, as well as the other parameters with no need to restart the process. This allow you to take an scheduled approach, e.g anneling.)

`& 1` is the way to make the square pattern of the checkerboard (it is like writing `% 2`). For example, in 1D, if `square_size` is 3, `grid // square_size` would be something like

`[0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3...]`

and `(grid // square_size) & 1` would be

`[0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1...]`

which is a one-dimensional checkerboard. It should be integer, not `bool`.

The `chunkDescriptor` line is not always just a purely hexadecimal number. If there are some extensions used, it may have some more stuff after a semicolon. If we are only interested in the number of octets for now then at least the `chunkDescriptor` should be split on `;` and the first value should be passed to the `int()` typecasting function or else be ready to face a `ValueError`. By extracting chunk size from chunk descriptor line, we can then set the termination condition of the while loop to compare with integer 0 instead of string 0.

[From Wikipedia:](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chunked_transfer_encoding#Format)

> Each chunk starts with the number of octets of the data it embeds expressed as a hexadecimal number in ASCII followed by optional parameters (chunk extension) and a terminating CRLF sequence, followed by the chunk data. The chunk is terminated by CRLF.

> If chunk extensions are provided, the chunk size is terminated by a semicolon and followed by the parameters, each also delimited by semicolons. Each parameter is encoded as an extension name followed by an optional equal sign and value. These parameters could be used for a running message digest or digital signature, or to indicate an estimated transfer progress, for instance.

> I just don't understand difference between `{'organization'}` and `{u'name': u'Library'}` parameters representation

The first argument specifies which fields nailgun should use when automatically generating a search expression for you. The second argument is merged in to the first - it allows for customization after a search has been generated. Here's a simple example of how the queries are merged:

```python
>>> from nailgun import entities
>>> org = entities.Organization(name='foo', description='bar')                                            
>>> org.search_payload()
{'name': 'foo', 'description': 'bar'}
>>> org.search_payload({'name'})
{'name': 'foo'}
>>> org.search_payload({'name'}, {'per_page': 50})
{'per_page': 50, 'name': 'foo'}
```

I will fully admit that the search generator currently *sucks*. It's awfully naive. But I'm OK with that - I know that we have the tools in hand to make it better, and I want us to put some effort in to doing so rather than writing manual search queries for the rest of our days.

After doing some research in depth, I believe this is still problematic. The reason is because the `get_task` function isn't protected by by the GIL See https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/450b11f1d69590332b9623f1043789aab4788bf7/src/local_scheduler/lib/python/local_scheduler_extension.cc#L77.

Here's the more detailed explanation of how things can go wrong:
1) the main thread executes the `get_task` function and switches out when it's in the middle of  `write_message`. (the request is half-sent now)
2) then the flush-profile-data thread also calls into `write_message` via `push_profile_events`, and also write data to the connection.
3) then the server will receive a corrupted request.
